data.title: Touchstone Small Company Fund Q3 2025 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Touchstone Small Company Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Review

The Touchstone Small Company Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.

Among the Fund’s top-performing sectors in terms of adding value were Financials and Consumer Staples. Stock selection and sector allocation positively contributed to Financials, while no allocation to Consumer Staples benefitted relative performance. The Fund’s bottom three performing sectors that detracted from relative performance were Health Care, Materials, and IT. Stock selection detracted in all three sectors. From a factor perspective, the Fund’s focus on profitable, liquid, and higher quality small cap stocks negatively impacted relative performance during the quarter.

Among the largest contributors to Fund performance were Ciena Corp. (IT sector), Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (IT sector), Nextracker Inc. (Industrials sector), Maximus Inc. (Industrials sector), and WNS Holdings Ltd. (Industrials sector).

Ciena (CIEN) is the global leading provider of optical network equipment. It was the top-performing stock last quarter as well. The stock returned 79% in the third quarter due to strong demand for their products to connect artificial intelligence (AI) data centers.

Tower Semiconductor (TSEM) operates foundries that manufacture specialty analog semiconductors. The stock returned 67% during the quarter after providing a strong outlook for revenue and earnings growth, driven primarily by their RF Infrastructure business that supplies chips used in data center connectivity.

Nextracker (NXT) is the global leading supplier of utility-scale solar arrays. The stock returned 36% after reporting record bookings and backlog. Additionally, the One Big Beautiful Bill lifted policy uncertainty for the industry and is supportive of continued investment in utility-scale solar, which added more than twice as much global electricity generation as any other source in 2024.

Maximus (MMS) is a strategic partner to the government delivering services to support programs such as Medicaid, Medicare, and Veterans health. The stock returned 31% after reporting record earnings and lifting their outlook, providing evidence of their critical services to the government and not being a focus of DOGE.

WNS Holdings provides business process management solutions to global corporations. The stock returned 18% during the quarter after announcing they were being acquired by Capgemini. Our large overweight positively contributed to the contribution to return. We exited the position due to the acquisition.

Among the largest detractors from Fund performance were Haemonetics Corp. (Health Care sector), SPS Commerce Inc. (IT sector), Silgan Holdings Inc. (Materials sector), LiveRamp Holdings Inc. (IT sector), and Option Care Health Inc. (Health Care sector).

Haemonetics (HAE) is a global medical device manufacturer with leading positions in plasma collections for biopharmaceutical, and blood management and interventional technologies for hospitals. The stock declined 35% after reporting a significant deceleration in growth in their third-largest business, interventional technologies. Strength in their two larger segments outweighed that weakness leading to the company reiterating their earnings outlook for the year. We added to the position on weakness.

SPS Commerce (SPSC) operates the largest network connecting retailers and their suppliers to optimize supply chain operations. The stock declined 23% after lowering their long-term growth targets to the high-single-digit range due to macroeconomic pressures on their customers due to weaker consumer demand and tariff uncertainty. We added to the position on weakness.

Silgan Holdings (SLGN) is a global supplier of packaging products for consumer food, beverage, and personal care products. The stock declined 20% during the quarter after reporting softer volumes in sports beverages and vegetable cans, which caused them to guide earnings to the lower end of their target range. We added to the position.

LiveRamp Holdings (RAMP) is a leading data collaboration partner for global brands and media platforms to digitally and securely share information used in advertising. The stock declined 18% after reporting lower than expected growth in annual recurring revenue. Despite the lower growth, the company’s earnings were better than expected, and they raised their full year earnings targets. We added to the position.

Option Care Health (OPCH) is the nation’s largest provider of home and alternate site infusion services. The stock declined 15% despite reporting record results and raising their revenue and earnings outlook. The market is concerned about the growth profile in 2026 due to the air of uncertainty over the pharmaceutical industry. We added to the position.

During the quarter, new purchases included Kulicke & Soffa Industries Inc. (IT sector), Certara Inc. (Health Care sector), and Gulfport Energy Corp. (Energy sector).

Kulicke & Soffa (KLIC) is a global leader in semiconductor technology. We believe after a two-year-plus downturn, the global broad-based semiconductor market is in the early stages of a cyclical recovery, which will drive capacity utilization and demand for the company’s products. Additionally, the company has innovative technologies in emerging secular growth markets.

Certara (CERT) is a leading provider of bio simulation software and services utilized in global drug development. We believe growing adoption of their solutions, including as a result of policy changes such as reduced animal testing, will accelerate revenue and earnings growth.

Gulfport Energy (GPOR) is an oil and gas exploration and development company with acreage in one of the highest returning and strategic shales in North America, the Utica Shale. We believe demand for natural gas will increase driven by power generation requirements and liquid natural gas exports, fueling higher prices and higher free cash flow at Gulfport.

Sales during the quarter included Apple Hospitality REIT Inc. (Real Estate sector), Kite Realty Group Trust (Real Estate sector), EMCOR Group Inc. (Industrials sector), Verint Systems Inc. (IT sector), and Viant Technology Inc. (IT sector).

Apple Hospitality (APLE) is a hotel real estate investment trust we sold due to concerns about weakening hotel demand. Kite Realty Group is a retail real estate investment trust we sold due to concerns the retail real estate market was at peak, with signs of increasing bankruptcies and higher vacancies.

EMCOR Group (EME) is one of the nation’s largest electrical and mechanical engineering and construction firms. The stock returned over 500% during our holding period the last three-plus years as demand for their services exploded driven first by the cyclical recovery post-pandemic, then by onshore investments in industries such as semiconductors and pharmaceuticals, and most recently by the massive AI infrastructure build-out. The stock appreciated above our market cap range and was added to the S&P 500, and as a result we exited.

Verint (VRNT) provides customer experience software to large global corporations and is being acquired by private equity. Viant Technology operates a demand-side platform that connects global advertisers and publishers. We exited the position due to competitive concerns.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of small-cap companies, which may be subject to more erratic market movements than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Fund invests in foreign securities which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund may focus its investments in a particular industry and/or market sector which may increase the Fund’s volatility and magnify its effects on total return.

Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change. The Advisor engages a sub-advisor to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-advisor who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisors. Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the Fund. To obtain a prospectus or a summary prospectus, contact your financial professional or download and/or request one at TouchstoneInvestments.com/resources or call Touchstone at 800.638.8194. Please read the prospectus and/or summary prospectus carefully before investing.

Touchstone Funds are distributed by Touchstone Securities, Inc.

A registered broker-dealer and member FINRA and SIPC

A member of Western & Southern Financial Group

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Italian Confidence Data For November Sends Contrasting Signals
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli, Senior Economist, Italy, Greece

November confidence data shows a clear dip in consumer sentiment alongside a broad-based improvement for businesses, which is still consistent with a modest return to positive GDP growth in the fourth quarter of the year

November confidence data shows an uneven picture: consumer sentiment is deteriorating while business confidence improves – except in construction.

Consumer confidence falls sharply

In November, consumer confidence fell by almost three points, reaching the lowest level since May 2025, dragged by deteriorating evaluations on both the economic situation and personal conditions, and affecting current and future developments.

Respondents repeatedly expressed growing anxiety about potential unemployment in the future. A decline in both the opportunity to save indicator and the opportunity to purchase durable goods signals tightening budgetary positions, suggesting that consumers are adopting a more prudent approach. This is not a good omen for private consumption in the fourth quarter.

Manufacturers are more upbeat, as order books gradually improve and stocks deplete

Confidence in manufacturing increased for the fourth time in a row, reaching the highest level since July 2023. The monthly gain reflects an improvement in domestic and foreign order books and a decline in stocks of finished goods; expected production increased consistently, without spurring expectations of higher future employment.

Manufacturing appears to be slowly emerging from stagnation; however, a decisive shift will only occur once the anticipated boost from German fiscal policy takes hold.

Confidence slightly down in construction, but the residential component remains resilient

On the construction front, confidence improved in the residential component; it has held at very high levels in civil engineering and softened in specialised works. The relative strength in the sector has been fuelled by the steady flow of infrastructural works funded by the resilience fund, but also by a surprisingly resilient residential component, which is absorbing the phasing out of the superbonus tax incentive better than expected.

Services confidence edges up again

Confidence in services was up on the month, reaching the highest level since January 2025. The improvement was driven by solid gains in tourism services and services to businesses. However, confidence was down in information and communication. All in all, the sense of fatigue which had affected services over the first quarter of 2025 is gradually disappearing. Services should be able to support economic growth in the fourth quarter.

To sum up, November confidence data provides a mixed picture of the state of the Italian economy in the fourth quarter. From the supply-side angle, a more balanced pattern seems to be in the making, with manufacturing gradually becoming a potential growth engine in support of services.

From the demand-side angle, confidence data seems to point to lingering softness in consumption and to confirmed strength in investments.

This still fits our base case forecast of a return to small positive quarterly GDP growth in the fourth quarter and average growth of 0.5% for the whole of 2025.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vistra Has Earned Its Premium, But There's Little Margin For Error
data.description:
Taking another look at Vistra (VST). My view hasn’t changed since the last time. The stock has dropped over 10% since August, from $197 to about $176. That’s after people pushed the price up to an all-time high of $219 in September. There were a lot of good feelings about the company’s big moves: buying up new power plants, expanding its nuclear power, and making deals. But then came the drop, and the company couldn’t stay at those highs. To me, that shows the market is starting to realize both the upsides and the risks. The way the price moved isn’t just people taking profits. It shows that it’s not as clear if the stock can keep going up. People are starting to wonder if the company can do even better from here.

I still don’t lean one way or the other on VST and think any bargain in the price is mostly gone. The price expects the company to do everything right with all its projects. It’s trading as if everyone’s already counting on the company’s 2027 profits. The stock is still up more than 26% this year, even though it’s been weaker lately. It’s not clear anymore if the possible reward is worth the risk for someone just buying in. Good news, like new projects doing better than expected, is now harder to surprise people with. And the risk of things going wrong with projects or in the market is bigger now. It makes sense to wait instead of putting in more money.

Business Snapshot

What makes Vistra strong is how it mixes two businesses. It runs a big power generation side, about 44,000 megawatts in gas, nuclear, coal, solar, and batteries, and also sells electricity and gas to about five million customers. That brings in steady money from its retail business and has a chance for more profit when wholesale power prices jump. The company keeps making itself stronger and trying to last longer, like with its Energy Harbor merger, which added more nuclear power and more customers, and the $1.9 billion Lotus deal for more natural gas plants.

These moves try to make Vistra a key player as the power grid gets more complicated and pushes to use less carbon. For example, it signed a 20-year contract for its Comanche Peak nuclear plant, locking in steady money and helping smooth out some of the ups and downs in the business. It also just opened a big solar plant in Texas and is looking at ways to squeeze more power out of its nuclear sites.

But there are bumps in the road. The company lost a chunk of battery storage after a fire and had another fire at a coal plant that’ll keep one unit out of service until late 2025 or early 2026. Insurance pays for a lot of the costs, but these problems are still tough to plan for. Running a big, complicated mix of power plants means things can go wrong.

Market Context

It’s actually a pretty good time to be a big U.S. power company. Demand is rising with all the new data centers, electric vehicles, and factories moving back to the U.S. At the same time, there’s growing pressure to cut back on coal and gas. Vistra operates in some of the biggest power markets, like ERCOT and PJM, so it can really benefit when power prices spike. Its mix of nuclear and renewables also helps it qualify for state and federal incentives. That said, the government could also change rules again, which could either help or hurt, and the company has to keep adjusting. Plus other big companies want a piece of the same pie: Constellation, NRG, and more. If Vistra slips up, on a project, with a rule, or keeping customers, someone else will take their spot.

Financials

The numbers look good at the top line. Vistra brought in $4.97 billion in the third quarter and $13.15 billion so far this year. That’s a tiny drop from last year because of some outages and higher costs. Operating profit is down a lot, mostly from higher costs and a few asset write-downs, down from $2.59 billion to $1.04 billion this quarter and to $1.43 billion from $3.48 billion year to date. EPS is down this year; the drop in EPS this year comes from costs going up, plant problems, and write-downs. Margins are still strong for a utility. The company’s return on equity is strong, but that’s partly because it uses a lot of debt. Cash from its business is solid, $2.64 billion so far this year, but less than last year. The company’s capital return program remains active, with recent dividends declared on both common and preferred shares. They’re spending a lot on new projects and repairs, too. Debt is high at $18 billion, so if profits drop further, things could get tight.

Valuation

Vistra trades at about 26.7 times 2025 expected earnings. Constellation goes for a lot more, about 38 times earnings. NRG’s multiple is lower at 21. So that puts Vistra slightly below the average of this group. Still, this doesn’t mean the stock is cheap. At this valuation, the stock already bakes in good news. People are counting on steady growth and no big problems. If things get rough, or if Vistra misses on earnings again, the price could easily slide down toward $130. Given the balanced risk and rewards at these levels, I think Hold makes sense.

Sentiment

People’s feelings about the stock have changed from feeling great in the fall to a more cautious mood now. Big firms still say to buy it, but one already cut its rating in September, saying good news is already baked in. News about fires at plants reminds everyone that big, complicated companies can still have problems. Press releases about new deals and regular dividends are positive, but I think people are now looking more at what’s already happened than what could come next.

Risks

The risks the company talks about match what I worry about. Rules could change, fuel costs could jump, or something could go wrong at a plant. Having so much debt helps when things go well, but makes problems worse if profits drop. If things don’t go as planned, the company might have to change its plans about paying shareholders or spending on new projects. Buying and building so much at once means more chances for things to go wrong. And even though management has mostly been careful, there’s just a lot on their plate.

Outlook

Looking ahead, most people expect sales and profits to keep growing for the next couple of years as Vistra finishes new projects and gets more out of its recent deals. The key things to watch are if Vistra can get new projects working, make the targets it’s promised, and keep costs in line. Good surprises could come from demand being even higher, government support, or keeping costs down. But any problem, big or small, over the next year or so will be the real test. If something goes wrong, even if the company fixes it, the stock could drop a lot.

Scenarios

If things go wrong, like shutdowns, higher costs, or tougher regulations, profits could fall 15–20%, and the stock could easily drop to $130 or lower as people start worrying more about the debt and get pessimistic. If things mostly go according to plan, profits of $6.8 to $7.6 billion in 2026 could support a stock price around $150 to $170, with growth and payouts helping offset the debt load. And if everything breaks their way—higher power prices, no major hiccups, and some helpful news from the government—profits could top $8 billion, and the stock might reach $200 to $220. But there’s very little room for mistakes, and the risk is still high.

Final Take

To sum it up, I’m sticking to my Hold call. Vistra is a top company in its industry. Management is bold and has delivered a lot. But right now, I think the market is maybe too in love with the company’s story and not worried enough about what could go wrong. There’s some upside, but there are real risks too. I think it’s smart for investors to wait for either a real pullback or some new, unexpected reason to buy. If you already own it, hold on, but don’t buy more of it right now.

data.title: RDVI: An S&P 500 Option Strategy That Misses The Mark
data.description:
I previously covered an interesting income strategy leveraging options to get additional dividends over its benchmark: the WisdomTree Equity Premium Income Fund (WTPI) here with a buy rating. Mixing the characteristics of a well-recognized benchmark with a smart derivatives strategy can be a way to deliver investors with superior risk-adjusted returns, or to boost the income distribution without hurting total returns.

Today, I will look at the FT Vest Rising Dividend Achievers Target Income ETF (RDVI), which provides investors with exposure to dividend risers of the S&P 500 with an option overlay to boost income metrics. However, I do think that while the strategy of the fund might be interesting, its reference benchmark doesn’t really make sense with the strategy, and it would be better applied to the S&P 500 directly. I rate the fund a hold because its approach, while aimed at conservative income investors, results in a fund that is riskier than a simple investment in the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO).

Yes, the income distribution of the fund is high, but this is a case of income trap: by privileging income, you are hurting your total returns without reducing risks.

Dissecting The "Target Income" Strategy

One surprising thing about the fund’s two layer strategy is that these two layers are not aligned. The equity portion of the fund is tracking the NASDAQ US Rising Dividend Achievers Index, following the performance of S&P 500 companies that “have increased their dividend value over the previous three year and five-year annual periods, while being best positioned to continue the dividend increases.”. In front of that, to generate additional income, the fund is selling call options based on the S&P 500. Owning a portfolio of “value” companies and selling the volatility of a “growth” portfolio is a significant mismatch.

If the S&P 500 rallies up, which happens from time to time because of the predominant share of growth companies in the index, not included in the portfolio of RDVI, the short call position of the fund will create losses that the fund’s portfolio companies are not going to be able to compensate for. RDVI is set for a stagnating or decreasing S&P 500. I like contrarian approaches, and maybe you will find it relevant for hedging purposes in your own portfolio. But as a standalone income investment, this is a very risky position. With a primary goal of “provide investors with current income with a secondary objective of providing capital appreciation”, I don’t understand the choice of the benchmark index. It absolutely does not make sense if your objective is income: the 30-day SEC yield of RDVI is 0.63% against 1.09% for VOO. The same strategy applied to the S&P 500 directly would provide investors with stronger income, and a better potential of capital appreciation.

Focusing on companies with rising dividends when their aggregate dividend is not matching that of the market, and barely compensating the 0.75% expense ratio of the fund, is surprising. The dividend yield of the fund is 8.44%, meaning that the option strategy is responsible for a dividend of 7.81%. I could understand this positioning if the goal was to limit the exposure to growth themes, but the fact that the fund is selling options based on the S&P 500 and that its objective is clearly about distributions and capital appreciation indicates that this is not the case. The mismatch is intentional, and the fund is designed for a struggling stock market, this is why we are going to see very poor historical performances.

Paying Premium Prices For Underperformance

As I mentioned before, RDVI is very expensive with an expense ratio of 0.75%, which is a 0.72 percentage points premium over VOO’s 0.03%. Cost-discipline for an income vehicle is very important, and RDVI is not doing very well on this point. As a reference, WTPI that I mentioned earlier had an expense ratio of 0.44%. It is very hard to rationalize paying such a high expense ratio, and this is why I don’t think that investing in RDVI is a good idea.

A justification for the price would be the addition of the "Rising Dividend" strategy over a cheaper ETF like WTPI, which should act as a quality filter. I have only one metric to convince you that this is not the case: the portfolio turnover. For RDVI it is 78%, against 2% for the S&P 500 ETF. For an ETF that invests in companies with a multi-year track record or rising dividend, it is very surprising to see that the average company does not stay in the portfolio for much longer than a year. This goes to show the low level of conviction linked to the strategy, and it also pushes costs up.

The strategy of the fund is not necessarily bad, but the underlying benchmark makes no sense with the strategy. A pure play on synthetic income would yield more consistent returns and be cheaper.

Sector Bets

If we look away from the option strategy to look at the equity portfolio (but not key holdings due to the high portfolio turnover), we will see the following sectoral split, well concentrated on a few industries. The fund is not concentrated in terms of individual holdings as its top 10 accounts for 25.09% of the portfolio against 40.11% for VOO, but it is in terms of sectors, as you can see from the table below.

Sector

RDVI

VOO

Difference

Financials

28.13%

+15.23

Technology

26.60%

-9.50

Industrials

+10.64

Consumer Discretionary

15.50%

10.50%

+5.00

Health Care

-5.46

Energy

+0.24

Telecommunications

0.61%

10.10%

-9.49

As you can see, RDVI’s equity positioning is centered around 4 sectors.

Financials: 28.13% of the fund. The outlook for the industry is positive thanks to increased M&A activity and yield curve sensitivity. This is a source of volatility amid concerns on the Fed's next moves, creating a complex landscape for banks with a wide array of projections about net interest margins. Lower interest rates are going to boost the demand for credit, but lower margins: it is hard to predict what will be next for the sector.

Technology: Aligned with unfolding market trends but underweight against the market, the tech allocation is capturing AI-driven investments and IT spending. This sector is a source of capital gains, but the lower than the S&P 500 exposure could be an opportunity cost.

Industrials & Consumer Discretionary: Together making for a third of the portfolio, these two sectors are also sensitive to economic trends. This is a solid positioning if the soft-landing hypothesis holds, but it could translate to losses if the economy slows while other companies of the S&P 500 in sectors traditionally seen as riskier have now amassed a significant cash pile.

The allocation of RDVI is clearly meant to be defensive, but you will see in the following part that this has not been a winning bet, and its growth potential is not high enough to compensate for the option strategy implied losses (especially during the past year).

The Illusion Of Safety

While it is not an explicit goal of the fund, an income strategy using a dividend index as its benchmark is aimed at conservative investors with the promise of stable returns. The issue with RDVI is that the fund is highly volatile and delivers poor risk-adjusted returns. The strategy is destroying value, and in addition, you have to pay a lot for it.

The fund is fairly new with an inception in late 2022 so we only have a short track record. Over the last three years, the standard deviation of the fund was 15.62%, above the S&P 500’s 12.87%. This means that the strategy of RDVI creates additional risks against the market. This is not a problem by itself, the issue is that the fund is doing so by lowering the total returns of the S&P 500. For this reason, if you look at risk-adjusted returns, there will be a clear gap. The Sharpe ratio of RDVI is 0.73 against 1.28 for the S&P 500. This is nearly a 2x difference, indicating that you are very poorly remunerated for the risks taken.

The reason behind that is that the fund’s strategy is creating a very asymmetric profile. The call-writing strategy is limiting the upside and not protecting investors in case of a market crash because of the underlying equity exposure. During a market crash, the portfolio of RDVI made of cyclical sectors (Financials and Industrials) will follow, even if companies that have been rising dividends for 3 to 5 years are expected to be of higher quality than the rest of the equity universe (this is a very naive approach to the markets that you know is not working if you read my previous articles on such strategies).

This is all very clear when comparing total returns:

RDVI is always behind (except in the very-short term, but this is because of the volatility), and the fund might only be relevant if we enter a recession.

In addition to that, the liquidity of the fund is not very good. The fund’s average daily trading volume is $12.2 Mn (0.49% of the fund’s AUM of $2.5 Bn), resulting in a 0.08% median bid/ask spread. During a market crash, you can expect it to widen a lot.

Conclusion

The FT Vest Rising Dividend Achievers Target Income ETF is an example of what an income trap is: an investment where an apparently high dividend yield comes at the expense of capital gains, resulting in a low total return with regard to the risks taken.

The mismatch between the fund’s underlying equity benchmark and the index it uses for its option strategy is creating a basis risk that results in underperformance.

data.title: Cheery Earnings, Leery Investors And An Optimistic Equity Outlook
data.description:
By Carrie King

Q3 company earnings offered a lot to cheer, though market action leading into year-end has featured a mix of emotions as positive earnings compete with AI skepticism. Equity CIO Carrie King believes the AI influence is enduring and sees growing opportunity for stock selection as the market increasingly seeks to discern potential AI winners and losers.

U.S. stocks, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, are on pace for 14% growth in earnings for Q3 2025. This marks the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth and comes in well ahead of analysts’ Sept. 30 estimates of 7%.

The “Magnificent 7” stocks were standouts once again, growing earnings per share (EPS) 21% in the quarter compared to 13% for the “other 493.” Our data shows 85% of companies beating their EPS estimates, though market reaction showed a smaller price reward for beats and greater punishment for misses than historically has been the case. This is likely a reflection of the market’s high expectations after what’s shaping up to be a third consecutive year of healthy gains.

Against this backdrop ― and amid recent whiffs of AI consternation ― we offer three reflections anchored in the most recent quarter’s earnings:

Even “wow” comes in degrees

Within the context of an exceptional quarter at the index level, there were clearly leaders and laggards. Even in those sectors that wowed, we saw notable differentiation.

Of the four S&P 500 sectors that have driven performance year-to-date (utilities, communication services, IT and industrials), all but one notched Q3 earnings growth above the Mag 7. Communication services was the exception, weighed down by disappointing results from a Mag 7 constituent.

The upshot: Even the top tier features differentiation. Dispersion crosses sectors, themes and the individual stocks within them, and this means exciting potential to generate alpha through skilled stock selection.

The top-earning sector this quarter was IT, boasting 40% EPS growth. Over 90% of companies beat revenue estimates and almost all (98%) exceeded EPS growth estimates. Yet from a return perspective, our analysis as of Nov. 14, 2025, found a 305% difference between IT’s top and bottom performers.

AI the defining line

Each of the S&P’s top-performing sectors of the year has a distinct AI influence. Likewise, AI was the defining line between leaders and laggards within these sectors.

In IT, we observe the picks and shovels of the AI buildout disrupting traditional software and services. In utilities, we see a preference for areas likely to grow amid AI data center energy needs, including nuclear, renewables and gas. And in industrials, we find that companies enabling the AI revolution are preferred over cyclically exposed transportation companies. Power providers and manufacturers of cooling systems and components reported large jumps in orders in the quarter on AI data center demand.

This type of nuance and dispersion speaks to the importance of deep bottom-up analysis to fully understand the business case and fundamental strength of each company considered for investment.

While recent talk of a market bubble has caused a pullback in the AI trade and the stocks of key beneficiaries, we remain optimistic. AI is a multi-year investment theme poised to create new business models across industries, and its strong performance is not based on hope but on fundamentals. As shown in the chart below, the return of tech and some other AI-exposed sectors has been driven more by earnings than multiple expansion. Our analysis found the earnings contribution to be even greater among the AI-powered tech leaders. As AI progress advances and more use cases emerge, we expect it to continue to drive differentiation throughout the market as some business models thrive while others are disrupted.

AI outperformance backed by fundamentals

Return composition of S&P 500 sectors, 2025 to date

Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities with data from FactSet, Oct. 31, 2025. Past performance is not indicative of current or future results. Indexes are unmanaged and it is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Opportunity beyond AI

Our optimism isn’t only about AI. Other standouts in the third quarter earnings season included financials (23% EPS growth and 90% of companies beating EPS forecasts), where ytd returns also have been driven by earnings (see chart above). We see further tailwinds supporting the banks, including potential for deregulation that already seems to be boosting M&A activity, and a generally healthy credit outlook, with stability in loan books and delinquency rates.

In industrials, though AI-adjacent companies retain the upper hand, we saw improving orders among cyclically exposed segments and above-consensus earnings growth that could be signaling better times ahead. The sector has performed well this year (see chart) as valuations appear to be pricing in good news to come.

The story was different for consumer companies, which collectively had a difficult earnings season. We still see bifurcation between the stressed lower-income consumer and mid- to higher-income counterparts and a value-seeking mindset overall. While “tariff” mentions on earnings calls fell this quarter, according to data from FactSet, 79% of consumer staples calls still made mention, the highest of all sectors. Pre-ordering of inventory may have softened some tariff impact to date, as company margins have held up well, but we are watchful for any change here and look to the holiday shopping season for more clues on consumer health.

Year-end market reflections

U.S. stocks endured a series of worries in 2025, starting with DeepSeek-related AI fears in late January, the “tariff tantrum” in early April, intermittent macro-induced ebbs and flows, and more recent concern of an AI bubble. Yet through each quarter, company earnings growth held steady or improved. We offer two broad reflections as we kick off the season of good cheer:

  1. Stock prices move much faster and more frequently than company fundamentals. Having a solid understanding of those fundamentals can offer some solace amid inevitable bouts of volatility.
  2. Corporate America demonstrates a unique dynamism that we believe provides the resourcefulness and elasticity to weather changing markets. But of course, it also represents a fantastically heterogeneous group of opportunities that we believe is best navigated with a fundamental active approach to investment.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon's Weak 2025 Sets Stage For 2026 - Buy
data.description:
Amazon's Lagging Performance Is Your Buying Opportunity For 2026

It's a common practice among institutional money managers to rotate capital throughout the year among the most liquid stocks - usually within the S&P 500 index - following their theses on relative valuation, end market shiftings, competitive advantages/disadvantages, etc. Over the past 5 years, we have seen such kinds of inflows and outflows, with previous laggards becoming performance leaders in a very short period of time.

[2024 performance]

So far this year, there's only one Magnificent-7 stock that delivered below 5% return, well below SPY's 15% gain - it's Amazon (AMZN):

To me, it's a great opportunity to buy this underperformance in AMZN because its fundamental profile has only gotten stronger, and the developments they've made on the AI front seem to be underestimated by the market, similar to what we used to see with Alphabet (GOOG) last few years. Next year, in 2026, I'm expecting to see AMZN as one of the strongest stocks among mega-cap representatives, hence my "Buy" rating confirmation today.

Why Do I Think So?

Unfortunately for my previous update on the stock, AMZN hasn't rebounded to the levels I was seeing for it. But the firm's finances haven't worsened. In fact, we saw another double-beating versus consensus in the third quarter, with revenues of $180.17 billion (+14.55% YoY) and adjusted diluted EPS of $1.95 (+36.36% YoY) exceeding the consensus views by 1.35% and >25%, respectively.

Their EBIT would have risen to $21 billion (vs. factual $17.4 billion in Q3) without FTC and severance charges because their AWS business kept gaining EBIT margin sequentially from 2Q 2025. AWS's backlog reached $200 billion at the quarter-end (October bookings alone exceeded all 3Q bookings combined, per the management's comments), with ARR hitting about $132 billion. The AI workloads are growing "three‑times faster than cloud at this stage of its development, according to Argus Research's coverage update on the stock (proprietary source), and I believe that AWS's full‑stack approach gives it a great competitive advantage over most other firms.

They've got the infrastructure (Trainium and Nvidia GPUs), platform services (Amazon Bedrock), and their own family of foundation models (Nova), all of which are constantly improved and optimised. For example, BofA analysts expect the see new updates at AWS's re:Invent forum in early December, especially regarding Trainium3, which is going to reportedly offer ~2x the compute of Trainium2 at ~50% better energy efficiency. For reference, Trainium is now a multi‑billion‑dollar business that went up by 150% QoQ in 3Q 2025, so Trainium3 is very likely to extend that growth momentum well into the next year.

Plus, at the upcoming event, we should see news on AWS's expanded Nova capabilities and deeper OpenAI integration after OpenAI's $38 billion multi‑year compute commitment to AWS was announced in early November. All this gives me some conviction that we're still in the early innings of Amazon's AI product cycle, and the upcoming event in a few days might make it more obvious to a broader group of tech investors.

Further speaking about Trainium3, I think the strategic point here is that Amazon is reducing its dependency on third‑party GPUs at the margin while still partnering deeply with Nvidia (NVDA). This should allow them to 1) control their own roadmap and unit economics and 2) offer differentiated price/performance to large AI customers. Unlike hyperscalers like Google and Meta (META), who diversify into TPUs and custom accelerators, Amazon's decided to build its own stack, which looks like a solid alternative choice. According to Cryptorank, AMZN is extensively testing NVDA chips' alternatives at its chip lab in Austin, Texas, so I'm really excited to see what AWS can roll out next week.

Rami Sinno, an executive, is the engineer in charge of chip design and testing. He is reported to have worked in the chip industry for decades. According to reports, he helped create the first two generations of Amazon AI semiconductors. Reports say that the Amazon chip lab in Austin, Texas, is packed with Amazon AI chips that compete with those of Nvidia.

The company is reported to have invented the cloud computing business fifteen years ago. Over time, Amazon started building the infrastructure that sustains it.

Another catalyst that I expect to lead to a narrative change around Amazon is the ever-expanding AWS Top Secret, Secret, and GovCloud regions. Seeking Alpha News recently reported that Amazon seeks to invest $50 billion into "AI and supercomputing capabilities for Amazon Web Services, or AWS, U.S. government customers", giving agencies direct access to Bedrock, SageMaker, Nova, Trainium, and Nvidia infrastructure behind the same security boundaries where Palantir (PLTR) currently operates. I think that these AWS sub-segments can potentially chip away at Palantir's addressable pie at the margin over the next few years, and even if it's more about redirecting new AI workloads than ripping out existing Palantir deployments, the opportunity for AMZN stock's repricing looks massive to me in case they manage to achieve some progress on that. For greenfield mission systems where the requirement is primarily scalable AI infrastructure, it becomes much easier for procurement teams to stay "inside the AWS stack" rather than layering a third‑party analytic platform on top, which seems true in domains that Julia Ostian (another Seeking Alpha analyst) alluded to when discussing Kiro and AI agents:

Companies won’t have other choice but to get AI assistants for coding, because they won’t be competitive otherwise. This is exactly what I predicted years ago with Palantir. I said that once companies adopt a productivity-enhancing technology, their competitors will have no choice but to adopt it as well. And this will happen with Kiro, or with Kiro alternatives. Companies cannot afford to be three to five times slower than their competitors. Large enterprises will accelerate adoption because they will see direct savings. Amazon already reported that migration tools built on these technologies saved customers 700,000 hours of manual effort, which is the equivalent of 335 developer years.

So, when people say we only see a supply but don’t see a demand for AI, I really don’t understand what more they would like to see as proof.

Another AI implementation is the AI-powered customer service agent that Amazon uses itself and saves a good money by doing so. Every AI agent reduces the workload on human reps, increases the speed of resolution, increases customer satisfaction, and restores enormous amounts of cost efficiency.

Source: From Julia Ostian' article on AMZN

I know that Palantir's core moat in the national security ecosystem is less about raw compute and more about ontology, and AWS Top Secret/Secret/GovCloud are primarily infrastructure and platform layers. But the line between "infrastructure" and "application" seems to be getting blurrier every quarter as Amazon pushes more data services, AI agents, and domain‑specific toolkits into these secure regions, expanding its TAM to the areas it didn't serve before. It's not a direct AMZN/PLTR comparison, though. I'm just assuming that AMZN can get at least some part of PLTR's valuation premium in the next few years.

To date, AMZN doesn't look cheap at all. It's trading at 32.5 forward P/E, which is significantly lower when we zoom out and see the past historical figures, but it's still a lot to me on an absolute basis.

And AMZN's free cash flows are falling sharply because of the rising CAPEX needs, so it presses the valuation and makes the current multiples quite fragile, prospectively.

But with the change in AMZN's narrative that I assumed above for 2026, I don't think that the stock should see its P/E ratio go too far below 30x (on a forward basis). Also, we should remember that AMZN's ROIC has climbed to ~16.5%, up from ~3% in 2016, so this fact justifies some sort of stability to the current P/E multiple in the next couple of years.

Anyway, at 30x, and assuming that the beating track record continues further, and AMZN records a 15% premium on FY2027 EPS, the resulting price target would amount to ~$270.48 per share. This is a 12-month upside potential of over 18%, which can be even bigger if AMZN gets to a higher P/E next year.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

My thesis is based on specific catalysts that may not play out the way I expect them to. For example, in case Amazon's AI projects move more slowly to production/monetisation, or if its large customers increasingly standardise on rival stacks like Azure/OpenAI, or Google/TPU, AWS's AI workloads will no longer continue to grow at the past elevated pace. Plus, the heavy CAPEX they've spent on AWS Top Secret/Secret/GovCloud might run into slow procurement cycles and ultimately lead to very low ROIC.

Also, there's a core assumption from my end that next year, there should be a capital rotation to AMZN. But in reality, if markets decide to massively rotate out of "AI winners" on bubble fears, Amazon's FWD P/E ratio could easily de‑rate below my assumed ~30x, especially if any of the other issues/risks materialise at the same time.

The Bottom Line

I know that the risks of buying AMZN for >30x forward earnings seem to be high, but the company's business growth and existing moat are enough to justify this premium, in my opinion. In fact, I believe the premium can rise higher than that in the future if the company finds new TAM pockets (in the government area, for example), or if they can demonstrate that Trainium3 can meaningfully shift AI demand onto its own silicon stack. I see an upside of over 18% in the next 12 months, but Wall Street is even more bullish, seeing over 28% in AMZN's growth potential at the midpoint.

Based on all the above, I decided to keep my "Buy" rating on AMZN unchanged for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: FBY: Turning Meta's Pullback Into Weekly Income
data.description:
Overview

After assessing many YieldMax funds over the last year, I've been able to measure the performance, use cases, and payout sustainability through different market environments. With that said, I believe that these high-yield funds are best utilized as an accompanying position for investors that already have an established position in the underlying equity a fund tracks. YieldMax META Option Income Strategy ETF (FBY) aims to generate large double-digit distributions from its option strategy, while indirectly mimicking the daily price movement of Meta Platforms (META).

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FBY's share price has fallen by 30.86%. Unfortunately, these declines are typical across the YieldMax suite. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 1.87% over the same time frame. While this may seem like lackluster performance, I believe that a fund like FBY has a very specific use case. FBY now has a starting dividend yield around 37.7% and issues payouts on a weekly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation.

When I previously covered META, I issued a hold rating due to the fact that META traded near all-time highs at the time. I felt like it didn't make sense to initiate a position in such a high-risk fund since I suspected a pullback at some point. Now that META has retreated from its all-time highs, FBY's share price has fallen significantly in the last few months. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's potential and utility going forward.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, FBY has total net assets of $146.3M and has a gross expense ratio of 0.99%. FBY is actively managed as the fund seeks to generate weekly income from selling call options on META. However, the caveat is that FBY implements a synthetic option strategy, which simply means that FBY doesn't actually own any common shares of META. Therefore, the fund is writing options against an asset that it doesn't actually own, which can increase the overall risk profile and price deterioration.

As we can see from the holdings below, FBY is primarily backed by U.S. Treasury bills. Additionally, FBY has an active call option that expires on November 28th, while another call option for $650 per share expires in January of 2026. Since FBY implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing strategy, this helps the fund capture some upside growth through market rallies. However, the growth is ultimately limited to whatever the selected strike price is.

I currently own twelve different YieldMax funds, and the reality is that these large distributions are simply unsustainable. Funds like FBY are essentially self-liquidating, but it's by design. The only scenario where FBY's share price will see its share price increase over time is if META experiences a strong rally that happens rapidly. This can be driven by a strong earnings report, new product developments within the AI space, or even just the overall momentum of the technology sector. Despite this weakness, FBY is still a solid choice for investors that are looking to capitalize on the uncertainty and volatility of META's stock price.

META recently reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were strong. For instance, total revenue for the quarter amounted to $51.24B, which represented a 26.2% year-over-year increase. However, EPS landed at $1.05 per share, which caused META to miss estimates by $5.66 per share. The caveat is that EPS was impacted by a one-time tax-related charge, so the strength of earnings seemed to be a bit misinterpreted. Additionally, META pulled back from its highs due to concerns over the company's large capex spending. For reference, META confirmed $600B in capex spending to build out AI infrastructure in the US.

As we can see below, META's share price has declined by more than 15.7% over the last three months. As a result, FBY's share price has declined by more than 20.6% over the same period, and I believe this is the perfect opportunity to add shares.

Performance Insights

Since the option strategy limits the growth potential for the fund, FBY is likely to underperform META over time. Therefore, investors need to understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time. Just to demonstrate this, we can measure FBY's performance since its inception. FBY's share price has declined by approximately 37.75% since inception. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 61.75% over the same time frame.

After observing the performance of these single-stock YieldMax funds, I believe that a position in FBY makes the most sense as an accompanying position. For instance, META sits as one of my top holdings, and I plan to maintain a long-term position as I am bullish on the company's future growth and its ability to participate in the growing demand across the AI sector. However, I believe that the way upward will be filled with volatility and uncertainty, which is the environment for FBY. I believe the fund's performance outlook can be summarized in three scenarios:

  1. META's Stock Price Rises = FBY is likely to see minimal price increases, and the distributions may increase as the option premiums are larger.
  2. META's Stock Price Is Flat/Choppy = FBY's share price may trade within a consistent range as long as META doesn't experience any rapid declines. The distributions may also remain within a consistent range, which can lead to outperformance.
  3. META's Stock Price Declines = Depending on the severity of the pullback, there's a good chance that FBY will erode investor capital and distributions may get smaller over time.

With these concepts in mind, I believe that FBY makes the most sense if you are bullish on the underlying movement of META in the near term. When measuring FBY's performance since inception, it's clear that the META's outperformance gap will continue to widen over time, assuming that earnings continue to grow. This is why it makes the most sense to utilize FBY as an accompanying position.

In the future, I plan to revisit FBY to build out a more direct comparison to Roundhill META WeeklyPay ETF (METW). The fund has an inception dating back to June of 2025, so the operating history is very short, and it wouldn't make sense to complete an in-depth comparison yet. METW offers a ~41% dividend yield and also pays out on a weekly basis. The main differentiator is that METW doesn't utilize options. Instead, METW uses leverage to gain 1.2x exposure to the underlying shares of META. When running a quick performance comparison, we can see that FBY has outperformed so far.

Dividend Risk & Utility

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.0904 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 37.7%. Despite the high yield, investors should be aware that new payouts are declared every week, so there's a chance that the estimated annual yield will be different by the time you read this. Since payouts are determined based on the success of the written options, momentum of META, and the implied volatility levels, the distribution amounts will vary every week. Therefore, FBY is not the best choice for investors seeking a consistent payout range.

Additionally, the dividend history may look skewed because the fund previously paid out distributions on a monthly basis. The weekly frequency is a new system that was implemented in October of 2025. This change was made simply to cater to the growing demand for weekly income generation amongst investors. My main concern is that FBY's payouts will slowly deteriorate over time. Since the fund's large yield typically translates to a declining NAV, the payouts are typically adjusted as well. Therefore, FBY may not be the simplest buy-and-hold fund and may require investors to reinvest portions of their distributions to keep payouts similar over time.

However, we are talking about a dividend yield above 37%. This means there are some creative uses for the income received. Personally, I hope to maintain a position for long enough until I reach house money status, which is the cross point where an investor receives their initial investment back in distributions. For instance, a $10,000 investment is currently estimated to produce ~$3,778 in annual income and will help investors reach house money status in less than three years. This is a simplified approach to the concept, which assumes that payouts will remain within a consistent range.

$10,000 investment / $3,778 annual income = 2.64 years.

At the time of my last coverage, I stated that the distributions are typically tax-efficient due to their reliance on return of capital. However, it's important to note that these payout methods can change over time. For instance, the most recent payout was only made up of 25.85% of return of capital, which indicates that the remaining portion of the payouts was likely fueled by net investment income.

So if you want to limit the potential tax liability from FBY, a position would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account. Maintaining exposure to FBY within a regular taxable account can lead to higher than anticipated tax consequences for investors. When it comes to the utility of distributions, I believe that the weekly frequency allows us to implement a dividend wheel method. For instance, I currently reinvest a portion of my weekly distributions into unrelated growth positions that are attractively valued. This helps offset any losses from these higher-yield income funds.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on FBY to a buy due to META's recent pullback. I believe that FBY is an efficient way to capitalize on the volatility and uncertainty of the price movements in the near term. However, FBY's price is still likely to decline and underperform META over the long term. Therefore, I believe that the fund is best utilized as an accompanying position for investors that already have an existing position in META. If you are bullish on META, FBY is a great way to turn the market's uncertainty into weekly income.

data.title: Autodesk Gave Up Most After-Hours Gains To Close At Fair Valuation
data.description:
Autodesk (ADSK) surged 8% during after-hours trading following the release of FQ3 earnings yet quickly erased most of those profits to end the trading day with only 2% in the green. That was reason enough to have a deeper look at where the stock stands fundamentally and in the bigger valuation picture.

While this is my first time covering Autodesk on Seeking Alpha, I had already analyzed the software company as part of a German research series back in November 2023. At that time, we considered the stock worth buying out of a stage of undervaluation. This has resulted in a solid 49% gain to date, almost in line with the S&P 500. Let us now analyze whether this has now closed the stock’s prior undervaluation-gap.

GAAP Or Non-GAAP Valuation?

It is noteworthy that Autodesk’s GAAP and non-GAAP earnings and margins differ significantly from one another. The company’s Q3 operating margin, for example, was 38% on a non-GAAP basis and only 25% based on GAAP. The GAAP to non-GAAP EPS difference is similarly large. The most significant contributing factor to this difference is stock-based compensation, as highlighted below. Therefore, investors must first decide for themselves which of those earnings figures they want to build upon.

Looking at the company’s trading multiples, it appears that investors clearly focus on non-GAAP metrics. Otherwise, a GAAP P/E of around 60x would appear highly unjustifiable. Non-GAAP (adjusted) P/E, on the other hand, is only around 30x.

Under the assumption that Autodesk’s adjusted P/E ratio is the metric that best reflects the company’s valuation, the stock’s 10-year valuation average of 45x would at first glance suggest that the company is still cheap. However, we must acknowledge that this long-term average would include both times with negative EPS and periods of irrationally high valuations until 2021.

Therefore, it appears more reasonable to scale down the valuation average to a 3-year timeframe, during which the company’s adjusted P/E averaged 30x. Based on this approach, Autodesk would now be trading exactly at its fair value. This, along with some arguable ambiguity concerning stock-based compensation, prompts me to assign a rather neutral Hold rating to Autodesk at this point. However, a $390 price target for FY 2028 based on consensus estimates and a 30x assumption for adj. P/E would still imply adequate double-digit returns with Autodesk.

When looking at free cash flow relative to adjusted earnings, it becomes evident that over the long run, adjusted earnings are a good reflection of the company’s free cash flow, as stock-based compensation expenses do not lead to cash outflows.

Within shorter time frames, however, free cash flow was more volatile than adj. earnings, which was partly attributable to the company’s past practice of billing lump sums to their customers, which were not necessarily in-line with Autodesk’s revenue recognition.

Now the company has switched its billing practices, which should not only lead to more in-line earnings and cash flow developments going forward but also distort some reported figures during the transition phase. Therefore, we should mainly focus on Autodesk’s figures adjusted for these effects.

Focus On Adjusted-For-Transactional Changes

As for FY 2026, which will end in January 2026, Autodesk expects to have generated roughly $2.3 billion in free cash flow, implying a significant increase of 45% YoY, mainly due to the aforementioned one-time catch-up effect of faster revenue-to-cash conversion amid the transition of the billing logic.

Billings themselves are expected to reach $7.5 billion, up 25%, but only 20% adjusted for the new model, and revenues will be almost $7.2 billion, up 17% as reported but only 11% adjusted for the new model. What is now a transitional tailwind, will expectedly turn into a headwind in the next fiscal year, as the effects of the billing transition will no longer exist.

With that, we should continue to build upon management’s adjusted guidance in the 11% range for revenue growth. This is roughly in line with FY 2024 and FY 2025 YoY topline growth and represents a continued slowed yet solid double-digit pace compared to growth rates up to the high twenties in the years prior.

RPO Suggests Reliable Revenue

Another metric that I find helpful when analyzing Autodesk is putting its remaining performance obligations [RPO] in relation to its realized revenue, either on a TTM basis or based on the current quarter times 4. RPO includes both billed and unbilled deferred revenue and essentially provides insight into how much revenue is already partly waiting in the balance sheet to be released going forward. I emphasize “partly” because unbilled deferred revenue is not recorded in the books under the accounting standards applied, although it is reported to investors.

With the current RPO/Revenue ratio above 100%, at least one year of the company’s current annual revenue run rate is already waiting to be realized. Add to that the fact that 97% of Autodesk’s revenue is considered recurring, most of which comes from subscriptions. This shows the company’s expectedly stable income stream.

Why Autodesk Should Be AI Resistant

Finally, this article focused on some financial technicalities and provided a big picture overview of where the company stands in terms of the stock market, while other analysts have already provided valuable and deep insight into Autodesk’s core business areas and their respective macro environments. At this point, let us just briefly go over key aspects.

Autodesk is one of the most internationally diversified US software companies, which is an aspect I personally highly appreciate. On a TTM basis, 44% of revenues came from the Americas, growing 16%; 38% came from EMEA, growing 18%; and 17% came from Asia Pacific, growing 9%.

Note that these growth rates are unadjusted ones and include beneficial short-term effects from transactional changes. Autodesk’s largest segment, AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations), contributes 49% of revenue and grows the fastest at 20% on a TTM basis. The rest, including CAD software, manufacturing simulation, and media animations, grew mid-single to low-double digits.

Given the fact that Autodesk tools are intertwined into lots of highly safety-critical projects like infrastructure or construction, I do not see it easily being replaced by AI. While AI is becoming better at a fast pace, I believe it still has a long way to go until it is accurate and “accountable” enough to shoulder critical tasks like that. This provides ample room for Autodesk to continue to position itself as a trusted partner who responsibly incorporates AI into its own specialized tools.

Takeaway

Autodesk runs a high-quality software business that I do not expect to be easily disrupted by AI due to the safety-critical nature of the projects it is used in. The company rather stands at the forefront of gradually letting AI flow into its products. Q3 earnings essentially confirmed Autodesk’s long-term path. At 30x adjusted P/E, the stock now trades at its own 3-year valuation average. Given some arguable ambiguity due to share-based compensation not considered in this adjusted metric, I stick to a Hold rating now that the stock has made up for its undervaluation in recent years, expecting risk-adequate returns going forward.

data.title: Atour Lifestyle: Bullish On Beat And Raise Quarter
data.description:
I've stayed positive on Atour Lifestyle Holdings (ATAT).

Q3 headline numbers were above consensus. It is expecting a substantial revenue surge for the full-year period.

The previous June 26, 2025 article drew attention to the promising signs for ATAT's retail and hospitality operations.

Recent Performance Was A Pleasant Surprise

The firm published a press release revealing its 3Q25 financials on Tuesday, Nov 25.

ATAT registered a normalized bottom line of CNY 0.49B in Jul-Sep '25, implying a +27% jump on YoY terms.

As per S&P Capital IQ data, its latest quarterly net profit exceeded the sell-side's projection by 3%.

Both of the company's divisions did well.

My late-June update highlighted that ATAT's "initial foray into bedding was focused on pillows, so there are opportunities in related products." I also cited management's remarks that "comforters as a proportion of total bedding revenue can possibly go up from one-fifth in 2024 to half in due course."

The group's 3Q2025 retail-related turnover was +76% higher YoY at CNY 0.85B.

According to its results slides, ATAT "launched upgraded products of the Deep Sleep Thermo-Regulating Comforter series" in the third quarter of the current year. This drove up the product line's "accumulated (unit) sales" to over 2M.

Its efforts to provide a wider range of merchandise for retail clients have paid off.

Separately, the YoY "Revenue Per Available Room/RevPAR" contraction for ATAT's hotels narrowed from 2Q25's -4.3% to 3Q25's -2.2%.

I touched on the improved Q3 RevPAR for Atour's peer H World (HTHT) in a Nov 18, 2025 write-up. My analysis indicated that HTHT benefited from "the booming leisure travel trend" and a slower pace of "supply growth" in China's hospitality market. These are also tailwinds for ATAT.

Internally, it worked hard at growing its footprint.

ATAT noted at the analyst briefing that "we opened 152 new hotels (in 3Q2025), a record high for a single quarter."

Its group-level top-line rose 38% to CNY 2.63B for 3Q25. This was equivalent to a 7% outperformance versus consensus (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Also, ATAT's EBITDA-to-sales expanded 120bps (basis points) from 24.9% for 2Q2025 to 26.1% in the recent quarter.

The enterprise's profitability was boosted by a favorable change in revenue composition and excellent cost control.

Its earnings announcement disclosed that "revenues from our leased hotels for the third quarter of 2025 decreased by 13.4%" because of "our product mix optimization." ATAT's "leased hotels" have lower margins than its "manachised (managed/franchised)" properties, due to the latter's asset-light nature.

Atour's "General & Administrative/G&A" expenses-to-revenue metric declined by -0.8ppts (percentage points) YoY and -0.2ppts QoQ to 3.4% for 3Q25.

The company's future is also bright, as I will elaborate in the next section.

Management Has Become More Optimistic About Full-Year Prospects

ATAT's FY25 topline growth guide was lifted from "+30%" earlier to "+35%" post-results.

In my Jun 26 piece, I mentioned "ATAT's bedding brand called Yaduo Planet, or YP was among the top three in terms of homecare category sales at China's mid-May to mid-June 618 Festival (Black Friday equivalent). I also predicted then that "another upward revision to its full-year retail sales outlook" was on the cards, considering "how well YP has done."

Indeed, the group decided to change the retail unit's sales increase guidance for '25 to +65%. The prior August and May forecasts were inferior at +60% and +50%, respectively.

YP still has a long runway, taking into account the whitespace.

Atour's Sleep-Focused Offerings

As per the chart presented above, ATAT's retail-related business is penetrating new sub-segments such as "loungewear" and "fitted sheet."

Also, its results call commentary pointed to the intention of "building a product portfolio that covers different scenarios" with "products like Deep Sleep Travel Pillow and Deep Sleep Pillow for Children." It's clear that YP is targeting younger consumers and travelers to broaden its customer base.

Appealing Shareholder Returns Are Supported By Generous Cash Distributions

It's encouraging to know that ATAT has a balanced capital allocation approach that isn't solely about investing for future growth.

Atour made a commitment at the 3Q25 earnings briefing that it will be "targeting a (dividends/buybacks) payout ratio of 100% (previously 50%) based upon the previous fiscal year's GAAP net income."

The 2025 consensus earnings estimate for ATAT is $223M based on S&P Capital IQ. Its current market cap is $5.2B.

That translates into an FY25 stockholder yield of 4.3% (223/5200).

You Would Contemplate These Risks

A weak Chinese economy could lead to a drop in demand for the company's retail- and hospitality-related products/services.

ATAT has pegged its capital return to a proportion of net income, rather than outlining an absolute figure. A lower-than-expected bottom-line will also likely translate into disappointments relating to repurchases and dividends.

My Conclusion

I continue to award a 'buy' rating to this name.

ATAT's EBITDA multiple has the potential to re-rate by +33% (16/12), using its listed comparable as a benchmark.

The consensus next twelve months' EV/EBITDAs for Atour and HTHT are 12x and 16x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

A healthy growth outlook and an attractive shareholder yield justify richer valuations for ATAT.

data.title: Blackstone: A High Beta Stock Now At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This year’s third quarter of Blackstone Inc. (BX) was relatively weak compared to Q3 2024 primarily due to a sharp drop in Performance Allocations (carried interest perceived by Blackstone GPs in investment funds) due to headwinds in private equity and high stock market volatility. I believe nothing is fundamentally broken in the Blackstone model. Interest rate cuts (next one expected in December by the markets) and monetary easing will likely bring in massive capital injections and revive the alternative investment world.

The current dip in the stock price is an excellent entry price, as investments are likely to pick up again as capital is injected into the system. The change of Fed Chair in May, from Powell to Trump’s new pick (identity to be determined still), will also likely increase the probability of a Fed that goes in easing mode.

Business Overview

Blackstone is a leading asset management firm in the alternative space, and its revenue depends primarily on the AUM (from which fees are derived) it has, which grows as new products or funds are launched, but also based on the performance of their investments. The company has exposure to the entire alternative space, from private equity to infrastructure, from real estate to private debt and even to the nascent institutionalisation of cryptocurrencies. Quite naturally, the stock’s beta is significantly higher than 1, at around 1.75, as the company’s earnings are directly correlated to market liquidity, investor optimism and asset performance. With a presently rather restrictive monetary policy and relatively high-interest rates, the alternative sector, in particular private equity and real estate, have overall seen a slowdown in the dynamism and number of transactions since 2021. High treasury yields and strong stock market performance in the past years have also taken some liquidity from the alternatives. However, despite the slowdown, Blackstone has been able to consistently grow and, despite a somewhat mixed picture in the most recent earnings report, I believe the current dip is an ideal entry point.

Recent Earnings

Net income is flat on a January-September basis at around $4.1 billion in 2024 and 2025, give or take. However, on an LTM basis, net income is up +28%, which confirms the earnings growth picture. The issue is where the picture starts becoming less rosy: revenue in Q3 2025 is sharply down (-20%) relative to Q3 2024 despite relatively strong stock market performance. Quarterly revenue dropped from $3.7 billion to $3.1 billion, approximately.

Most of the revenue loss is in the line “Performance Allocations” which is roughly the carried interest perceived by GP interest and usually calculated based on their funds performances. This means Blackstone-led funds have underperformed significantly the S&P 500 in the same timeframe. I dove deeper in the financials supplement to understand if the Blackstone model is somehow broken: if it is, then the issue would be across all the segments where Blackstone has GPs.

The good news is that one segment alone has been a huge drag in the investment performance of Blackstone: real estate. The good news is the remaining segments have performed relatively well on a LTM basis and contributed to revenue and earnings with fees in rising AUM. Real estate has naturally seen its valuations drop (and arithmetically Blackstone’s real estate AUM) where mortgage rates in the US exceed 6% p.a. Interest rate cuts and capital injections should in theory lower real estate financing rates and bring some dynamism back to the segment in 2026. This could be a tremendous tailwind for Blackstone that, nonetheless, continues to show strong growth rates.

Valuation

With a revenue growth rate of 14.8% YoY and ROCE of 35.2%, Blackstone can be considered a growth stock. Its growth rates have consistently been in the double digits since years for both the top and bottom lines. And for a growth stock in the Financials segment, I believe the stock is now attractively valued after a 27% correction in its price in the last 12 months and -18% YTD.

The stock is now trading at a stark discount to its 5-year average. The stock is trading at a P/E of 26x based on full-year 2025 earnings (vs. 28x on its 5Y average) and a P/E of 22x on 2026 earnings, reflecting monetary and real estate tailwinds. The forward P/E is at a 20% discount to its 5Y average forward P/E and could lead to a strong rally leading into 2026. The stock’s beta of around 1.75 could lead the stock significantly higher as the stock market is rebounding now after last week’s dip, potentially fueled by a resuming of monetary easing. Comparing with peers such as BlackRock Inc. (BLK) that is trading at 19x 2026 earnings and KKR & Co Inc. (KKR) at 17x 2026 earnings, Blackstone does look expensive. But when you put the ROCE in perspective, Blackstone’s 35.2% far exceeds Blackrock’s 12.6% and KKR’s 8.9%, which in my opinion justifies the premium valuation.

Risk Factor To My Case

The key risk of this high beta stock is if inflation heats up again, causing a postponement of the Fed’s monetary easing and hence headwinds on asset valuations. This scenario is not probably at the moment despite recent scares due to a lack of data following the US government shutdown. Nonetheless, the stock can rapidly drop if the stock market needs to price high-interest rates for longer, again. The best way to mitigate this key risk is to incorporate the stock in a well-diversified investment portfolio oriented for the long term.

Bottom Line

Bottom line: Blackstone is a high beta stock with strong fundamentals and high-growth rates. The recent dip is an excellent entry point (or accumulation point, if, like me, you also already own the stock). Given the tailwinds I expect in 2026, I rate the stock a Strong Buy as long as the allocation is reasonable, within a sufficiently diversified portfolio to mitigate the inherent volatility of the stock.
